Then, H is a supporting hyperplane of the epigraph of f at (p,f(p)). Because (p,f(p)) belongs to the relative interior of the line segment joining (p1,f(p1)) and (p2,f(p2)), these two points also lie in H. Therefore, v ∈∂f(p1) and v ∈∂f(p2), which implies that ∂f(p1) ∩∂f(p2) ̸= ∅. Conversely, suppose that there exist two different pointsp1 and p2 in Csuch that ∂f(p1)∩∂f(p2) ̸= ∅. E. Forward-Correction Loss In this appendix, we verify that a forward-corrected losslW conforms to Theorem 7. A weak-label loss lW : P(Z)×Y→ R is called the forward correction of lYif lW(q,y) = lY(Tq,y), where lY: P(Y) ×Y→ R is a proper loss for estimating weak-label posterior probabilities. We ﬁrst apply Theorem 5 to lYand ﬁnd that lY(q,y) = −[∇F∗ Y(q)]y + FY(∇F∗ Y(q)) for q ∈P(Y); here, F∗ Y(q) is the negative Bayes risk corresponding to lY, and FY(v) is its convex conjugate. The latter can be inverted to ﬁnd that ∇F∗ Y(Tq) = RT∇S(q) −∆(q) for some function ∆(q) that takes values on coker T. It also holds that FY(∇F∗ Y(Tq)) + F∗ Y(Tq) = ⟨Tq,∇F∗ Y(Tq)⟩ (69) = ⟨q,∇S(q)⟩= S∗(∇S(q)) + S(q), (70) where the ﬁrst and third equalities follow from the equality condition of the Fenchel-Young inequality (Rockafellar, 1996). By using S(q) = F∗ Y(Tq) in Eq. (70), we ﬁnd that FY(∇F∗ Y(Tq)) = S∗(∇S(q)). Experimental Details Datasets We used the MNIST (LeCun et al., 1998) and CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky, 2009) datasets. Each dataset deﬁnes its training and test splits. In our experiment, we split the training split into two splits: one was used for training, and the other was used for validation. Table 3 lists the numbers of examples in the dataset splits. Before starting the experiments, we converted the ground-truth labels in the training splits into complementary labels. A complementary label for an instance was randomly chosen with probabilities given by the transition matrix in Eq. (18). Training procedure We used stochastic gradient descent with momentum to optimize the models. The momen- tum and the mini-batch size were ﬁxed to 0.9 and 256, respectively. The initial learning rates were chosen from {0.1,0.03,0.01,0.003,0.001,0.0003,0.0001}as those giving the best validation accuracy. When a learning rate of 0.1 or 0.0001 achieved the best validation accuracy, we also tried two more values beyond the predeﬁned range. In all such cases, we conﬁrmed that the chosen values were at a peak or on a plateau of the validation accuracy. The chosen values are listed in Table 4. The default value of the weight decay coefﬁcient is 10−4, but when it is tuned, it is chosen from {0.1,0.03,0.01,0.003,0.001,0.0003,0.0001}. Our proposed method, generalized logit squeezing (gLS), has two hyperparameters: the exponent αand the coefﬁcient k. For a ﬁxed α, we searched for the value of kthat achieved the best validation accuracy. The candidate values were 10, 3, 1, 0.3, 0.1, 0.03, and 0.01. These results are listed in Table 6. We adopted early stopping to determine the training time. Speciﬁcally, when the validation accuracy had not improved for the last 10 epochs, the learning rate was reduced by a factor of 10, and the third time the same condition was satisﬁed, the training was terminated. The test accuracy reported here is for the epochs with the best validation accuracy. Table 7 lists the numbers of epochs at which the best validation accuracy was achieved. We used a simple grid search strategy for the hyperparameter search. The best hyperparameters (i.e., the learning rate and the gLS coefﬁcient) were used in the evaluation step, in which a randomly initialized model was trained on the training split and evaluated on the test split. The training duration in the evaluation step was also determined by the early stopping strategy as described above. Other details All the experiments were performed using on-premise computation servers equipped with NVIDIA’s GeForce GTX 1080Ti and Tesla V100. The training duration varied signiﬁcantly, depending on the methods and the model size, but the longest run took less than one hour on the Tesla V100. We used PyTorch (Paszke et al., 2019) to implement the experiments.

llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Addresses weakly supervised classification under label corruption by establishing when weak-label losses are both proper (enable correct class-posterior estimation) and lower-bounded (avoid divergence). Introduces a dual representation theorem for proper losses (dual to Savage representation), characterizes T-proper weak-label losses via a convex function F and reconstruction matrix R (including a cokernel term), derives a sufficient condition for lower-boundedness of T-proper losses, and proposes generalized logit squeezing (gLS) that makes any proper weak-label loss lower-bounded without losing properness. Empirically demonstrates that gLS outperforms or matches baselines on MNIST and CIFAR-10 with complementary labels, highlighting the importance of properness and lower-boundedness.
llm_extracted_info.methodology:
- Theoretical framework: Convex analysis and Fenchel–Young duality. Derives a dual representation (Theorem 5) expressing any regular proper loss as l(q,z)=λ_F(∇F*(q),z) with λ_F(v,z)=-v_z+F(v), where F is a closed convex function and F* its conjugate.
- Characterization of T-proper weak-label losses (Theorem 7): l_W(q,y)=λ_{F,R}(∇F*(q),y)+Δ_y(q) with λ_{F,R}(v,y)=-(RTv)_y+F(v), R a left-inverse (reconstruction matrix) of the label transition matrix T, and Δ(q) in coker(T); Δ does not affect expected risk.
- Lower-boundedness: Shows standard proper losses (e.g., softmax cross-entropy) can become unbounded under weak labels; provides a sufficient condition (Theorem 13) that F(v)−max_y (RTv)_y is bounded below, equivalently F*(Re_y)<∞ for all weak labels y (Proposition 14).
- Regularization scheme (gLS, Corollary 15): Replace F by F'(v)=F(v)+ (k/2)∑_z |v_z|^α with α>1 to ensure superlinear growth, yielding T-proper and lower-bounded losses. Presents training pseudocode for BC+gLS.
- Unifies forward and backward corrections within the framework; shows how each fits the characterization.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup:
- Weak-label setting: Complementary labels (each instance labeled as NOT class c), transition T=(1/(K−1))(1_K−I_K).
- Datasets: MNIST and CIFAR-10. Training splits further split into train/validation; labels in training converted to complementary labels. Test on original test sets.
- Models: MNIST—linear classifier and MLP (1 hidden layer). CIFAR-10—ResNet-20 and Wide-ResNet-28-2.
- Methods compared: (1) BC: backward-corrected softmax cross-entropy (proper but unbounded). (2) BC+GA: BC with gradient-ascent trick to enforce non-negativity of partial risks (bounded but improper). (3) BC+gLS: proposed lower-bounded, T-proper loss with α>1 (α=2 by default; sensitivity to α and k studied).
- Optimization: SGD with momentum 0.9, batch size 256, grid search over initial learning rates; weight decay default 1e-4 and also tuned for BC and BC+GA; early stopping with LR reductions. 16 independent trials per setting; report mean±std test accuracy. Implementation in PyTorch; code link provided; GPUs used (1080Ti, V100).
llm_extracted_info.limitations:
- Assumes input-independent, known label transition matrix T and existence of a left inverse R (reconstructibility), requiring |Z|≤|Y|; may not hold in many real datasets.
- Sufficient (not necessary) condition for lower-boundedness; does not fully characterize all bounded T-proper losses, especially when Δ(q)≠0 (|Y|>|Z| cases).
- Empirical evaluation limited to complementary labels (extreme noise) on MNIST/CIFAR-10; lacks experiments on other weak-supervision regimes (e.g., general label noise, partial labels, PU) and larger/real-world datasets.
- Baseline comparisons focus on BC and GA variants; forward-correction losses and other robust methods are not empirically compared.
- gLS introduces hyperparameters (k, α); although some robustness is shown, practical tuning is still required and large k may underfit.
- Requires convex F and regularity assumptions; analysis does not fully address scenarios with input-dependent T(x) or unknown T estimation errors.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions:
- Extend theory and practice to input-dependent or unknown transition matrices T(x); develop estimation/joint-learning of T with guarantees under the proposed framework.
- Strengthen boundedness results to necessary-and-sufficient conditions; handle the Δ(q) term explicitly and study strict properness conditions operationally.
- Broaden empirical validation to diverse weak supervision types (noisy labels with varying rates, partial labels, PU, multiple noisy sources) and larger benchmarks; compare against forward-correction and other robust losses.
- Automate/adapt hyperparameter selection for gLS (k, α), e.g., via data-dependent schedules or bilevel optimization; analyze generalization and optimization dynamics under gLS.
- Explore connections to and differences from Lp regularization; study effects on robustness (e.g., adversarial), calibration, and uncertainty estimation.
- Investigate composite/link-function design leveraging the dual representation for improved optimization and calibration.
- Empirically evaluate the multi-source setting with heterogeneous noises as formalized in the appendix and integrate domain adaptation techniques.

new_method.method:
Motivation
- Problem: High‑dimensional (d≈10^2–10^5) estimation of specific stochastic‑Taylor/Lie‑bracket coefficients for E[g(X_T)] under x,θ‑dependent diffusions is computationally prohibitive (dense O(d^2–d^3) derivative flows) and statistically brittle (variance explodes when isolating order‑K≤3 terms).
- Why now: Neural‑SDE training, high‑dimensional Greeks with local/stochastic volatility, and weak‑Taylor PDE fixes increasingly require order‑2/3 corrections and parameter sensitivities in regimes where diffusion ranks are low‑to‑moderate (effective r≪d), and where compute must focus on the SDE’s actual occupancy rather than uniform grids.
- Gaps: (i) Avoid dense spatial derivative flows when σ(x,θ) varies; (ii) directly target order‑K coefficients (not just values/gradients); (iii) allocate evaluations to few, high‑leverage time–state nodes; (iv) collapse variance with coefficient‑aware critics; (v) end‑to‑end bias/variance control with MLMC and principled time randomization; (vi) robustness to non‑smooth drifts.

Methodology: HiLo‑STeP++ (High‑dimensional Low‑rank Stochastic Taylor Probing)
Core principle: Estimate order‑K≤3 weak‑Taylor coefficients via Stratonovich Lie‑bracket functionals using only Jacobian‑vector products (JVPs), randomized bracket contractions, diffusion‑subspace restriction, and coefficient‑specific Poisson/BSDE control variates. Integrate an occupancy‑aware node selector (moment‑guided RPC) and MLMC coupling for bias–variance control.

1) Coefficient calculus and targeting
- Hall‑basis expansion: Express target weak‑Taylor coefficients for E[g(X_T)] in a minimal Hall basis of Stratonovich brackets of b and {σ_j} (e.g., [σ_i,σ_j], [b,σ_i], [σ_i,[σ_j,σ_k]]). Provide an automatic Itô→Stratonovich converter via directional JVPs to evaluate b^∘ = b − 1/2 Σ_j (∂σ_j)σ_j without forming dense Jacobians.
- Observable‑aware weights: A small library maps user‑supplied g to the required basis elements and their weights, returning both coefficient definitions and parameter sensitivities ∂/∂θ with the same JVP machinery.
- Bracket‑sparsity screening (new): Use a few Hutchinson/Hadamard probes to detect negligible bracket contractions at candidate nodes; prune basis elements adaptively before heavy evaluation.

2) Directional Lie‑bracket probing (DLP)
- JVP‑only recursion: Compute bracket actions via recursive JVP rules (à la Log‑NCDE), avoiding Jacobians/Hessians and reducing costs to batched JVPs.
- Randomized contractions: Estimate bracket contractions with Hutchinson/Hutch++/orthogonal Hadamard probes; share probe seeds across time and MLMC levels for strong coupling.
- Diffusion‑subspace restriction: Estimate local active diffusion subspace r via randomized range finding on empirical Gram of σ(x,t); restrict all probing to this r‑dimensional subspace to achieve O(m·r) per node.
- Probe alignment (new): Use a streaming Oja/Krylov update to align probes with top curvature directions of bracket contractions at selected nodes, cutting probe counts m to O(log r) under spectral decay.
- Krylov caching: Reuse small Krylov subspaces of differential operators along nearby nodes and across MLMC levels to amortize JVP costs.

3) Coefficient‑specific Poisson/BSDE control variates
- Per‑coefficient critics: For each coefficient with integrand h_c(t,x), train a critic c_ψ solving L c ≈ h_c (or BSDE counterpart). Use S_ψ = ∫ σ^T∇c_ψ dW as a zero‑mean control variate. Optimise ψ with detach‑style losses (no second derivatives) or a gradient‑network variant that approximates ∇c directly.
- Spectral preconditioning (new): Precondition critic training by projecting both L and h_c into the estimated diffusion subspace; apply a Neumann‑series preconditioner for ill‑conditioned L.
- Shared trunk, per‑head critics (new): Multi‑task critic architecture shares a backbone across coefficients and adds lightweight heads, exploiting correlation among h_c to reduce sample/compute.
- Steinised warm‑start: Stabilise early training with a score‑based Stein loss under the moment prior (see 4), avoiding second derivatives and poor early estimates.

4) Moment‑guided RPC over joint time–state
- Occupancy‑aware prior: Build a cheap Gaussian prior N(m(t),P(t)) for X_t via assumed‑density evolution (linearization or symmetric 3rd‑order cubature). Use it to whiten states for kernel definition.
- Bracket‑aware multi‑task RPC (new): Run RPC in the product space (t,x) with whitening by P(t), and use a block‑kernel that upweights locations with large proxy Var[h_c]; perform multi‑task RPC so one node set serves many coefficients with optimal weights from a small linear system. Increase nodes until an RPC residual tolerance is met.

5) Multilevel time randomization and drift tilting
- Time randomization: Convert path integrals to T·E[h(τ,X_τ)] with τ~Unif[0,T] to reduce variance and enable shared‑seed MLMC coupling.
- MLMC coupling: Couple Brownian paths and probe seeds across levels; adaptively allocate samples per level from online variance/bias estimates.
- Learned small tilts: Apply lightweight Girsanov drift tilting toward high‑leverage regions guided by bracket magnitudes; correct via Radon–Nikodym weights.

6) Robustness to non‑smooth drifts
- Light Gaussian mollification for piecewise‑smooth drifts (e.g., ReLU nets). Track total MSE as mollification + discretization + probing + quadrature errors; apply first‑order Stein de‑biasing under the moment prior when needed.

7) Plug‑ins and interfaces
- Generator‑gradient surrogate: Provide unbiased surrogates for ∇v and Hessians needed by generator‑gradient estimators, replacing O(d^2–d^3) state‑derivative flows with O(m·r) JVPs at a handful of RPC nodes.
- PDE solver hooks: Emit order‑K weak‑Taylor correction coefficients at RPC nodes to boost fixed‑step Kolmogorov PDE solvers.
- Parameter sensitivities (new): Return ∂/∂θ of each Taylor coefficient via the same probing infrastructure, enabling high‑dimensional Greeks and stable neural‑SDE training signals.

8) Theory and diagnostics
- Guarantees: Prove consistency of bracket‑coefficient estimators under JVP‑only randomized contractions; derive variance bounds under spectral decay of bracket contractions and diffusion‑subspace approximation error. Show zero‑variance limit when c_ψ≈u (Poisson solution), up to probing/discretization noise.
- MSE decomposition: MSE ≤ C1·(Hall trunc.)^2 + C2·(Δt)^{2p} + C3·RPC residual + C4·Var(probe)/N + C5·Var(CV misspec.) + C6·mollification^2.
- Complexity: With r the active diffusion rank and m=O(log r) Hutch++ probes, per‑node cost is near O(m·r) and total cost O(m·r·K·n_nodes); node counts controlled by RPC residual.
- Online controllers: Jackknife for probe‑count m; RPC residual for node budget; per‑coefficient CV gap to tune critic capacity; MLMC level allocation via sample‑variance estimates.

9) Implementation blueprint (PyTorch + functorch + torchsde)
- Bracket/JVP engine: Hall‑basis combinators up to K=3 with batched vmap JVPs and Krylov caching.
- Probe generators: Rademacher/Gaussian/Hadamard; Hutch++ with on‑the‑fly orthogonalization; probe‑alignment via Oja updates.
- Critics: Small MLP/CNN with detach/gradient‑network variants; diffusion‑subspace preconditioning; Stein regularizer under moment prior; streaming training during estimation.
- RPC: GPU‑friendly, whitened by P(t); multi‑task block‑kernel for coefficients; small linear solves with caching.
- MLMC: Coupled Brownian bridges and shared probe seeds; built‑in time randomization; adaptive level/sample selection.
- APIs: (a) order‑K Taylor coefficients and ∂/∂θ thereof, (b) generator‑gradient plug‑ins, (c) weak‑Taylor PDE correction coefficients.

How this advances beyond prior work
- Generator‑gradient: Eliminates dense x‑derivative flows by JVP‑only bracket probing with diffusion‑subspace restriction; adds coefficient‑specific Poisson/BSDE critics and multi‑task RPC over time–state—none present in generator‑gradient.
- BSDE‑based PDE solvers: Re‑targets the zero‑variance principle to individual Taylor integrands, not to values/gradients; provides second‑order‑free training (detach/gradient‑network) specialized per coefficient with diffusion‑subspace preconditioning.
- Log‑NCDE: Repurposes their Lie‑bracket JVP machinery for estimation, not modeling; adds occupancy‑aware RPC node selection, MLMC time randomization, and coefficient‑aware control variates.
- RPC/KQ/EZQ: First application to joint time–state node selection under SDE occupancy with multi‑task, bracket‑aware kernels; integrates moment ODEs purely as whitening/proposal (correctness does not assume Gaussian marginals).
- Assumed‑density FPK: Uses moment ODEs strictly for proposals/whitening and Stein de‑biasing; does not replace path sampling nor assume Gaussian correctness.

Evaluation plan
- Benchmarks: (i) High‑d Greeks with local/stochastic volatility and x‑dependent σ; (ii) neural‑SDE training (gradient/Hessian stability, train time to target loss); (iii) Kolmogorov PDEs with fixed steps (weak‑Taylor correction vs baseline error); (iv) synthetic SDEs with known Taylor coefficients to assess accuracy/bias.
- Baselines: Generator‑gradient, pathwise differentiation, BSDE critics (value/gradient only), simple MC without coefficient targeting.
- Metrics: Runtime scaling vs d and effective rank r; probe/node counts for target error; per‑coefficient variance/bias; gradient variance in neural‑SDE training; PDE error at equal timesteps.
- Ablations: JVP‑only vs explicit Jacobians; probe alignment; diffusion‑subspace restriction; multi‑task vs single‑task RPC; critic variants; MLMC/time randomization; drift tilting.

Expected impact
- Computational: Feasible K=2–3 stochastic‑Taylor estimation in d≈10^3–10^5 by turning O(d^2–d^3) flows into O(m·r) JVPs evaluated at a few occupancy‑selected nodes; further amortization via Krylov caching and MLMC.
- Statistical: Coefficient‑specific critics with diffusion‑subspace preconditioning approach zero‑variance limits; MLMC/time randomization stabilize residual variance and discretization bias; adaptive controllers yield automatic tolerance‑driven computation.
- Applications:
  • High‑dimensional Greeks under state‑dependent diffusions.
  • Low‑variance gradients/Hessians for scalable neural‑SDE training.
  • Weak‑Taylor corrections for PDE solvers at fixed steps in high d.
  • Higher‑order corrections to effective SGD dynamics near critical points without dense flows.
- Deliverables: Open‑source PyTorch library with APIs for coefficient/gradient estimation and PDE plug‑ins; reproducible benchmarks demonstrating scaling, accuracy, and variance reductions.

experimental_design.experiment_strategy:
Title: Experimental plans for validating HiLo‑STeP++ (K≤3 weak‑Taylor coefficient estimation via JVP‑only Lie‑bracket probing, multi‑task critics, RPC, MLMC)

Experiment 1: Synthetic linear/nonlinear SDEs with ground‑truth or high‑precision references
Objective and hypotheses
- Validate correctness and variance gains of coefficient targeting, JVP‑only bracket probing, diffusion‑subspace restriction, probe alignment, and coefficient‑specific critics.
- H1: Estimated K=2/3 coefficients reconstruct E[g(X_T)] with O(T^{K+1}) residual and near‑zero bias as Δt→0.
- H2: Runtime scales with d through r (active diffusion rank) and probes m≈O(log r), not dense O(d^2) flows.
- H3: Critics reduce variance by ≥10× on nontrivial coefficients; RPC cuts node count by ≥3× at fixed tolerance.

Models and observables
- Case A (commuting diffusions, sparsity check): d∈{256, 2k, 10k}, r=8. Itô SDE: dX = b(X) dt + Σ_j σ_j(X) dW_j with σ_j(X)=e_j f_j(x_j) (component‑wise functions), and b(x)=−Λx + c, Λ diagonal. Many brackets vanish: [σ_i,σ_j]=0 (i≠j). Observable g(x)=xᵀQx with Q sparse banded.
- Case B (non‑commuting, low‑rank linear, analytic reference for small d): d∈{16, 64}, r∈{2,4}. Stratonovich linear fields: b(x)=A x, σ_k(x)=B_k x. Then [σ_i,σ_j](x)=(B_i B_j−B_j B_i) x, etc. Choose g(x)=xᵀQx. Analytic E[x xᵀ] via Lyapunov ODE; also analytic bracket integrands via matrix commutators allow closed‑form coefficients at small T.
- Case C (nonlinear, high‑d scaling with MC reference): d∈{512, 2k}, r∈{4,8}. b(x)=−Λx + η tanh(Cx), σ(x)=U diag(s(x)) with U∈R^{d×r}, s_i(x)=√(a_i + b_i x_i^2). Observable g(x)=xᵀQx and a cubic g(x)=∑ w_i x_i^3 to exercise K=3.

Ground truth/reference
- Case A: Many coefficients provably zero; nonzero ones validated via high‑precision MC (1e7 samples, Δt=2^{−12}).
- Case B: Use matrix ODEs for mean/covariance to get exact E[g(X_T)] and, for very small T, exact weak‑Taylor coefficients from commutator formulae. Cross‑check against our estimates.
- Case C: Use MLMC with L=6 levels and N_L tuned for reference MSE < 1e−4 as gold standard for E[g(X_T)].

Compared methods
- HiLo‑STeP++ full: JVP‑only DLP + diffusion‑subspace restriction + probe alignment (Oja) + Hutch++ + multi‑task RPC + coefficient‑specific critics + time randomization + MLMC coupling.
- Ablations: remove each component in turn (no RPC, no critics, no subspace restriction, no probe alignment, no MLMC/time‑rand, single‑task critics, no bracket‑sparsity screening, naïve Gaussian probes).
- Dense‑Jac baseline (small d only): Explicit Jacobians/Hessians to form brackets and coefficients (demonstrates scaling and numeric stability limits).

Metrics
- Accuracy: Absolute/relative error of each coefficient and of reconstructed E[g(X_T)] using K=2/3 expansion; log‑log slope vs T to verify O(T^{K+1}).
- Variance: Sample variance of coefficient estimators; control‑variate reduction factor (CVR).
- Efficiency: Wall‑time to ε‑MSE; scaling vs d and r; number of JVPs; node count from RPC; probes m used; memory.
- Sparsity detection: Precision/recall for zero brackets in Case A after bracket‑sparsity screening.

Protocol
1) Implement JVP‑only bracket engine (PyTorch+functorch): Hall basis up to K=3; Stratonovich converter via JVP: b^∘(x)=b(x)−0.5 Σ_j J(σ_j)(x) σ_j(x) via directional JVPs.
2) Diffusion‑subspace estimation: At each candidate node, randomized range finder on empirical Gram of σ(x): draw q∈R^r probes, Y=σ(x)Q, do one power iteration; retain r̂ top cols; project probes.
3) Randomized contractions: Hutch++ with k=O(log r̂) Gaussian/Hadamard probes + Oja alignment updated per node cache.
4) RPC node selection in (t,x): Build moment prior N(m(t),P(t)) by assumed‑density evolution (linearization). Compute block‑kernel weights from proxy Var[h_c]; grow nodes until residual < τ.
5) Critics: Shared trunk MLP, per‑coefficient heads; loss: detach‑style Poisson residual with diffusion‑subspace preconditioning; Stein warm‑start epochs.
6) MLMC + time randomization: Express integrals as T·E[h(τ,X_τ)] with τ~Unif; couple Brownian bridges and probe seeds across levels; online variance‑based allocation.
7) Data sweeps: T∈{0.05,0.1,0.2}; d, r as above; m initialized at 8, adapted by jackknife.
8) For Case B small‑T, also compute analytic coefficients for [σ_i,σ_j] and [b,σ_i] contributions and compare term‑wise.

Key hyperparameters (starting points)
- RPC tolerance τ=1e−3 (Case A), 3e−3 (Case C); max nodes 256.
- Probes m_init=8; align_rate=0.1; Hutch++ sk=2.
- Critics: trunk width 128, 2–3 layers; head width 32; Adam 1e−3; 1–3k steps; Neumann precond depth 2.
- MLMC: base step Δt0=T/4, L up to 5; N_l from pilot runs.

Outputs/analysis
- Plots: error vs T slopes; variance bars per coefficient; runtime vs d; CVR; RPC nodes vs tolerance; m vs alignment on/off; sparsity PR in Case A.
- Success criteria: slopes match K+1 within ±0.2; ≥5× runtime improvement vs dense‑Jac at d=64; CVR≥10× on at least half of nonzero coefficients; correct pruning of ≥95% zero brackets with <1% false positives.

Implementation snippets (sketch)
- JVP Stratonovich correction:
  def strato_b(b, sigmas, x):
      corr = 0
      for sj in sigmas:
          v = sj(x)
          jvp = functorch.jvp(sj, (x,), (v,))[1]
          corr = corr + 0.5*jvp
      return b(x) - corr
- Lie bracket via JVPs: [V,W](x)≈J_W(x)V(x) − J_V(x)W(x) via jvp on W and V along each other, no dense Jacobians.
- Hutch++ contraction skeleton:
  def hutchpp_trace(op_mul, d, m):
      # op_mul: function R^{d×k}->R^{d×k}
      G = torch.randn(d, m)
      Y = op_mul(G)
      Q,_ = torch.linalg.qr(Y, mode='reduced')
      B = Q.T @ op_mul(Q)
      Z = torch.randn(d, m)
      return torch.trace(B) + (Z - Q@(Q.T@Z)).T.view(-1) @ op_mul(Z - Q@(Q.T@Z)).view(-1) / m


Experiment 2: High‑dimensional Greeks under local‑vol with low‑rank diffusion
Objective and hypotheses
- Demonstrate computational and statistical gains for parameter and initial‑value sensitivities (Greeks) in d up to 2k with r≪d.
- H1: For basket options with state‑dependent σ, HiLo‑STeP++ yields lower MSE/time than pathwise and LR estimators at equal compute, and scales with r not d.
- H2: Coefficient‑specific critics and multi‑task RPC reduce variance and compute ≥5× while preserving bias control via MLMC/time‑rand.

Model
- d assets S_t∈R^d, d∈{256, 1k, 2k}; r∈{3,5}. Dynamics (Itô):
  dS_t = diag(S_t) [ (μ − 0.5 v(S_t;θ)) dt + L(S_t;θ) dW_t ],
  where L(S;θ)=U diag(ℓ(S;θ)) with U∈R^{d×r} fixed factors (orthonormal cols), ℓ_i(S;θ)=σ0_i (1 + α_i tanh(β_i (log S_i − m_i))). v = diag(L Lᵀ).
- Observable: Basket call g(S_T)=max( (1/d)∑ w_i S_{i,T} − K, 0 ).
- Greeks: Delta along direction u (∂/∂ε E[g(S_T(1+εu))] at ε=0), Vega parameterized by θ=(α,β) vector; optionally rho wrt μ.

Compared methods
- HiLo‑STeP++: Use generator‑gradient surrogate to estimate ∇_θ of Taylor coefficients and reconstruct ∂_θ E[g] up to K=2 (and optionally K=3); use mollified payoff or conditional smoothing near kink.
- Baselines: Pathwise (with payoff smoothing ε→0), Likelihood Ratio (score function), generator‑gradient baseline if available, and naïve MC finite‑difference wrt θ (for small d only).

Metrics
- Accuracy: Relative error of Greeks vs high‑precision MLMC reference (Δt small, large N) for d≤256; for higher d report convergence vs compute and cross‑check on coarser dimensions.
- Variance/time: Variance per unit wall‑time; MSE at fixed time budget; scaling vs d and r.
- Stability: Gradient variance across seeds; sensitivity to payoff smoothing ε.

Protocol
1) Build simulator in torchsde with Stratonovich conversion when needed for bracket terms; couple Brownian paths across MLMC levels.
2) RPC over (t,S_t) using moment prior from lognormal approximation: m(t)=E[S_t] via geometric Brownian moments under local vol linearization; P(t) from assumed‑density evolution.
3) Coefficient selection: For Delta, target coefficients that most influence ∂/∂x E[g], guided by observable‑aware library; for Vega/θ use ∂/∂θ of the same coefficients via shared JVP infra.
4) Critics: Shared trunk with per‑Greek heads; precondition in diffusion subspace; Stein warm‑start for 500 steps; then train online for 2k steps while collecting samples.
5) MLMC+time randomization: L up to 5; sample allocation from pilot variance; share probe seeds across levels; optional light drift tilting towards large proxy |h_c| regions.
6) Compute Greeks with K=2 expansion and report error/time; repeat with K=1 to show gain from higher order.
7) Ablations: +/− critics, +/− RPC, +/− subspace restriction, +/− probe alignment, +/− MLMC/time‑rand.

Hyperparameters
- T=0.5; K=2 default; Δt0=T/4; ε_smooth for payoff: 1e−3 (anneal to 0 for reporting via Richardson extrapolation).
- RPC τ=2e−3; max nodes 128; m_init=8 probes; alignment on.
- Critics: trunk 256 width, 3 layers; head 64; cosine decay LR 1e−3→1e−4.

Outputs/analysis
- MSE vs time for each Greek and method; scaling plots vs d and r; CVR; node counts; probes used; gradient variance histograms.
- Success criteria: At d=1k, r=5, HiLo‑STeP++ achieves ≥5× lower MSE/time than best baseline; stable across seeds; benefit from K=2 over K=1.

Implementation sketch for Greek via coefficient reconstruction
- Compute coefficient estimates C_k and their θ‑derivatives dC_k/dθ.
- Approximate E[g]≈∑_{k=0}^K C_k; then ∂_θ E[g]≈∑_{k=0}^K dC_k/dθ.
- Use the same MLMC/time‑rand samples for both to reduce variance.


Experiment 3: Robustness under non‑smooth drifts with mollification and Stein de‑biasing
Objective and hypotheses
- Show that the method remains stable when drift is only piecewise smooth (e.g., ReLU network), where classical estimators degrade.
- H1: Light Gaussian mollification + Stein de‑biasing controls bias without exploding variance; critics further reduce variance.

Model
- d∈{128, 512}, r=5. Drift b(x)=W2 ReLU(W1 x) − λ x with W1,W2 random Gaussian (frozen); σ(x)=U diag(√(a + b ⊙ x^2)); Stratonovich needed for bracket logic.
- Observables: g1(x)=xᵀQx, g2(x)=tanh(vᵀx), and a non‑smooth surrogate g3(x)=max(uᵀx,0) smoothed with ε.

Methods
- HiLo‑STeP++ with:
  a) no mollification (control);
  b) Gaussian mollification x→x+ζ, ζ~N(0, s^2 I) inside drift evaluations only;
  c) (b) + Stein de‑biasing using score under moment prior N(m(t),P(t)) to cancel first‑order mollification bias.
- Baselines: Pathwise and LR estimators where applicable; generator‑gradient baseline if available.

Metrics
- Bias vs variance vs smoothing scale s; total MSE; robustness across seeds; computational overhead.

Protocol
1) For s∈{0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05}, estimate coefficients and reconstruct E[g(X_T)] (K=2) under fixed compute.
2) Apply Stein de‑biasing: fit score of N(m(t),P(t)) and add first‑order correction to remove mollification bias.
3) Train critics with Stein warm‑start to avoid second derivatives; measure CVR.
4) Ablations: With/without diffusion‑subspace preconditioning; with/without drift tilting to improve occupancy.

Hyperparameters
- T=0.3; Δt0=T/8; RPC τ=3e−3; m_init=10; critics trunk 128.

Outputs/analysis
- Plots: MSE vs s; variance vs s; bias vs s; effect of Stein de‑biasing; runtime overhead.
- Success criteria: With s≈0.02 and Stein de‑biasing, MSE within 1.2× of smooth‑drift baseline while no‑mollification fails (e.g., variance >10× or numerical instability), and CVR≥5×.


Common implementation details and diagnostics
- Libraries: PyTorch 2.x, functorch (vmap, jvp), torchsde for SDE integration (Stratonovich/Itô), numpy, matplotlib.
- Reproducibility: torch.manual_seed, fixed PRNG for Brownian paths and probe seeds; deterministic cudnn if using CNNs.
- Diagnostics to log per run:
  • Estimated active rank r̂ and spectrum decay at RPC nodes.
  • Probe count m selected by jackknife and variance estimates.
  • RPC residuals vs node count; node locations in time.
  • Critic losses (Poisson residual, Stein loss), CV gap.
  • MLMC per‑level variances, costs, and effective sample sizes.
  • Decomposition of MSE terms when possible: discretization, probing, CV misspecification, RPC residual.
- Risk mitigation:
  • Start with K=1, verify baselines, then enable K=2/3.
  • Validate Stratonovich converter on toy 2D examples.
  • For high‑d, profile memory; use checkpointing and mini‑batch RPC nodes.

Deliverables per experiment
- Clean scripts: exp1_synthetic.py, exp2_greeks.py, exp3_nonsmooth.py.
- Config files for d,r,T,K, τ, m_init, MLMC levels.
- Saved results (JSON/CSV) with metrics; plotting scripts.
- Ablation toggles via CLI flags.

Expected impact across experiments
- Demonstrate correctness (order slopes), scalability (r‑driven cost), and strong variance reductions from coefficient‑aware critics, RPC, and MLMC/time‑randomization; show robustness under non‑smooth drifts with principled de‑biasing.
experimental_design.experiment_details:
Title: Experimental plans for validating HiLo‑STeP++ (K≤3 weak‑Taylor coefficient estimation via JVP‑only Lie‑bracket probing, multi‑task critics, RPC, MLMC)

Overview and integration across experiments
- Goal: Validate correctness, scalability, and robustness of HiLo‑STeP++ using three complementary settings: (1) synthetic SDEs with analytic/high‑precision references; (2) high‑dimensional Greeks with local volatility; (3) non‑smooth drifts with mollification and Stein de‑biasing.
- Design principles for reliability and non‑overlap:
  • Shared core implementation (JVP bracket engine, diffusion‑subspace discovery, Hutch++ probing, RPC node selection with GP, multi‑task critics, MLMC/time randomization, optional tilting) with configuration toggles for ablations.
  • Each experiment focuses on a distinct stress: Experiment 1 emphasizes correctness and scaling; Experiment 2 emphasizes parameter sensitivities and application‑level MSE/time; Experiment 3 emphasizes robustness to non‑smoothness and de‑biasing.
  • Common diagnostic suite (variance decomposition, control‑variate gaps, RPC residuals, probe counts, active rank spectra) ensures consistent comparison across experiments while avoiding duplicated evaluation steps.
- Libraries: PyTorch 2.x; torchsde; BoTorch+GPyTorch (for RPC as GP‑based residual selection and multi‑task modeling); functorch or torch.autograd.functional for JVP; NumPy; Matplotlib; optionally torch.linalg for randomized range finding.
- Reproducibility: torch.manual_seed; fixed RNG streams for Brownian paths, probe seeds, and GP inducing point selection; deterministic cuDNN flags when needed.

Global implementation building blocks (used by all experiments)
1) JVP‑only Hall‑basis Lie‑bracket engine up to K=3
- Stratonovich drift converter b^∘(x) = b(x) − 0.5 Σ_j Jσ_j(x) σ_j(x) via directional JVPs (no dense Jacobians).
- Bracket via JVP: [V, W](x) = J_W(x)V(x) − J_V(x)W(x) computed by jvp on W and V along directions V(x) and W(x), respectively.
- Batched vmap for probes and RPC nodes.

Example code (assuming PyTorch 2.x; fall back to functorch if needed):

import torch
from torch.autograd.functional import jvp

# Vector fields are callables R^d->R^d; they may close over parameters/θ
def stratonovich_drift(b, sigmas, x):
    corr = torch.zeros_like(x)
    for sj in sigmas:  # sj: x -> R^d
        v = sj(x)
        _, jvp_val = jvp(sj, (x,), (v,), create_graph=False, strict=True)
        corr = corr + 0.5 * jvp_val
    return b(x) - corr

def lie_bracket(V, W, x):
    Vx = V(x)
    Wx = W(x)
    _, JW_Vx = jvp(W, (x,), (Vx,), create_graph=False, strict=True)
    _, JV_Wx = jvp(V, (x,), (Wx,), create_graph=False, strict=True)
    return JW_Vx - JV_Wx

# Example of nested bracket [V, [W, U]]
def nested_bracket(V, W, U, x):
    def inner(z):
        return lie_bracket(W, U, z)
    return lie_bracket(V, lambda z: inner(z), x)

2) Diffusion‑subspace restriction and probe alignment
- Local active diffusion subspace estimation at node (t, x): randomized range finder on σ(x) ∈ R^{d×r_full} using a few Gaussian/Hadamard probes.
- Keep top r_hat columns spanning range(σ); all bracket probing restricted to this subspace.
- Oja updates to align probe directions to dominant curvature of bracket contractions, reducing required probe count m.

Example code (randomized range + single power iteration + Oja update):

def estimate_diffusion_subspace(sigma_columns, x, q=16, power_iter=1):
    # sigma_columns: list of callable sigma_j(x) returning R^d vectors
    d = x.numel()
    r_full = len(sigma_columns)
    # Build matrix action S: R^{r_full} -> R^d by stacking sigma_j(x)
    S = torch.stack([sj(x) for sj in sigma_columns], dim=1)  # d × r_full
    # Randomized range finder on S
    Omega = torch.randn(r_full, q, device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
    Y = S @ Omega
    for _ in range(power_iter):
        Y = S @ (S.T @ Y)
    Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(Y, mode='reduced')  # d × q
    # r_hat from spectrum of S^T S projected
    B = Q.T @ S  # q × r_full
    U_s, Sv, Vt = torch.linalg.svd(B, full_matrices=False)
    # Choose r_hat by energy threshold
    energy = (Sv**2).cumsum(0) / (Sv**2).sum()
    r_hat = int(torch.searchsorted(energy, torch.tensor(0.99, device=energy.device)).item() + 1)
    Ur = (Q @ U_s[:, :r_hat])  # d × r_hat
    return Ur  # column‑orthonormal basis for local diffusion subspace

# Oja alignment for probes toward top curvature
@torch.no_grad()
def oja_update(probes, gradop_apply, lr=0.1):
    # probes: d × m; gradop_apply: function d×m -> d×m of symmetric op
    Y = gradop_apply(probes)
    probes = probes + lr * Y
    # orthonormalize
    Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(probes, mode='reduced')
    return Q

3) Randomized contractions via Hutch++ with subspace restriction
- Given a symmetric linear operator A (induced by a bracket functional or Gram), estimate traces or selected contractions using Hutch++ with orthogonalized probes, restricted to Ur subspace.

Example code:

def hutchpp_trace(op_mul, d, m=8, Q_init=None):
    # op_mul: callable R^{d×k} -> R^{d×k}
    G = torch.randn(d, m, device=next(op_mul.parameters(), torch.zeros(1)).device) if hasattr(op_mul, 'parameters') else torch.randn(d, m)
    Y = op_mul(G)
    Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(Y, mode='reduced')
    if Q_init is not None:
        # Blend with aligned probes
        Q = torch.linalg.qr(torch.cat([Q, Q_init], dim=1), mode='reduced')[0]
    B = Q.T @ op_mul(Q)  # small matrix
    # Residual part
    Z = torch.randn(d, m, device=Q.device, dtype=Q.dtype)
    PZ = Z - Q @ (Q.T @ Z)
    R = op_mul(PZ)
    trace_resid = torch.sum(PZ * R) / m
    return torch.trace(B) + trace_resid

4) RPC over time–state via multi‑task GP (BoTorch/GPyTorch)
- We model h_c(t, x) jointly for multiple coefficients c with an ICM multi‑task GP using a kernel on whitened (t, x) coordinates: k((t, x), (t', x')) = k_t(t, t') × k_x( (x−m(t))/P(t)^{1/2}, (x'−m(t'))/P(t')^{1/2} ) with automatic relevance determination.
- Node selection: greedy variance reduction. At each step, choose candidate that maximizes sum of posterior variances across tasks; stop when integrated posterior variance (proxy to RPC residual) drops below tolerance τ.
- Candidate pool: draw from occupancy prior N(m(t), P(t)) on a grid of times; optionally reweight by proxy Var[h_c].

Sketch code (ICM GP and greedy selection):

# pip install gpytorch botorch
import torch
import gpytorch
from botorch.models.multitask import MultiTaskGP
from botorch.fit import fit_gpytorch_mll
from botorch.acquisition import PosteriorMean
from botorch.optim import optimize_acqf

# Prepare training data (initial few points) X: N×(1+d), Y: N×C
# Whitening is applied before fitting

def fit_multitask_gp(X, Y, task_indices):
    # X: N×(1+d), task_indices: N (0..C-1), Y: N
    model = MultiTaskGP(X, Y, task_indices=task_indices, rank=1)
    mll = gpytorch.mlls.ExactMarginalLogLikelihood(model.likelihood, model)
    fit_gpytorch_mll(mll)
    return model

# Greedy selection from a candidate pool Xcand (M×(1+d))
@torch.no_grad()
def select_rpc_nodes(model, Xcand, K_add, tasks, tau, current_nodes):
    selected = []
    while len(selected) < K_add:
        post = model.posterior(Xcand, observation_noise=False)
        # Sum of marginal variances across tasks
        var = post.variance  # shape: M×C
        score = var.sum(dim=-1)
        idx = torch.argmax(score).item()
        selected.append(Xcand[idx])
        # Update training set with pseudo‑observation at mean to reduce variance
        # (equivalent to D‑optimal greedy; in code, actually refit or update fantasy model)
        current_nodes = torch.cat([current_nodes, Xcand[idx][None]], dim=0)
        # In practice use fantasy models (botorch.models.model_fantasize)
        if score[idx] < tau:
            break
    return torch.stack(selected, dim=0)

5) Coefficient‑specific critics (Poisson/BSDE control variates)
- Shared trunk with per‑coefficient heads; gradient‑network variant to avoid second derivatives.
- Train with detach‑style losses approximating L c ≈ h on collocation nodes (t, x) drawn from RPC. Diffusion‑subspace preconditioning: project gradient onto Ur.
- Use Stein warm‑start under moment prior to stabilise early training.

Sketch code (multi‑head critic with gradient head):

import torch.nn as nn

class CriticNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d, C, hidden=128):
        super().__init__()
        self.trunk = nn.Sequential(nn.Linear(d+1, hidden), nn.SiLU(),
                                   nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.SiLU())
        self.heads = nn.ModuleList([nn.Linear(hidden, 1) for _ in range(C)])
        # Optional gradient network head (predicts grad wrt x)
        self.grad_heads = nn.ModuleList([nn.Linear(hidden, d) for _ in range(C)])
    def forward(self, t, x):
        # t: N×1, x: N×d
        h = self.trunk(torch.cat([t, x], dim=-1))
        vals = [head(h) for head in self.heads]
        grads = [ghead(h) for ghead in self.grad_heads]
        return torch.cat(vals, dim=-1), grads

# Loss components
# L c ≈ h: Lc = b·∇c + 0.5 Tr[σσ^T ∇^2 c]; avoid Hessian by gradient‑network variant + Neumann preconditioner

def poisson_loss(critic, batch, coeff_targets, b, sigma_cols, Ur):
    t, x, h_targets = batch  # shapes N×1, N×d, N×C
    c_vals, grad_preds = critic(t, x)
    # project gradients to diffusion subspace
    Urx = Ur  # d×r_hat
    loss = 0.0
    for ci in range(h_targets.shape[-1]):
        grad = grad_preds[ci]  # N×d
        grad_proj = grad @ (Urx @ Urx.T)  # N×d
        # approximate Lc using projected gradients; 2nd‑order handled by Neumann or ignored initially
        drift_term = torch.sum(b(x) * grad_proj, dim=-1, keepdim=True)
        # simple surrogate for diffusion term via directional JVPs if needed (omitted for brevity)
        res = drift_term - h_targets[:, ci:ci+1]
        loss = loss + (res**2).mean()
    return loss

# Stein warm‑start: minimize E[(∇·(P^{-1}(x−m) c) + (P^{-1}(x−m))·∇c)] under Gaussian prior; implement with samples from prior

6) MLMC with time randomization and shared‑seed coupling
- Convert path integrals ∫_0^T h(t, X_t) dt to T · E[h(τ, X_τ)] with τ ~ Uniform[0, T].
- Use torchsde BrownianInterval to couple fine and coarse levels; share Brownian and probe seeds across levels.
- Adaptive allocation: choose N_l ∝ sqrt(V_l / cost_l) with pilot estimates; stop when variance and bias tolerances met.

Sketch code (level simulator and allocation):

import torchsde

class SDE(torchsde.SDEIto):
    def __init__(self, b, sigma_cols):
        super().__init__(noise_type='general', sde_type='ito')
        self.b = b; self.sigma_cols = sigma_cols
    def f(self, t, y):
        return self.b(y)
    def g(self, t, y):
        # stack columns into d×r
        return torch.stack([sj(y) for sj in self.sigma_cols], dim=-1)

@torch.no_grad()
def simulate_level(sde, x0, T, dt, seed):
    w = torchsde.BrownianInterval(0.0, T, shape=x0.shape, device=x0.device, dtype=x0.dtype, seed=seed)
    ys = torchsde.sdeint(sde, x0, torch.tensor([0.0, T], device=x0.device), dt=dt, bm=w, adaptive=False)
    return ys[-1]

# MLMC allocation after pilot

def allocate_mlmc(costs, variances, N_total):
    weights = torch.sqrt(variances / costs)
    nl = (N_total * weights / weights.sum()).clamp_min(10).to(torch.int64)
    return nl

7) Girsanov drift tilting (optional)
- Tilt drift by small δb toward high |h_c| regions; correct with Radon–Nikodym weight: 
  w = exp(∫_0^T σ^† δb · dW − 0.5 ∫_0^T ||σ^† δb||^2 dt). Implement with discretized sum along simulated path if used.

Sketch weight computation:

def girsanov_weight(sigmat, delta_b_path, dW_path, dt):
    # sigmat: function giving σ at path states; delta_b_path: Nsteps×d; dW_path: Nsteps×r; dt scalar
    # σ^† δb via least squares in diffusion subspace
    w_inc = []
    for k in range(delta_b_path.shape[0]):
        sigma_k = sigmat(k)  # d×r
        pinv = torch.linalg.pinv(sigma_k)
        z = pinv.T @ delta_b_path[k]  # r
        w_inc.append((z * dW_path[k]).sum() - 0.5 * (z @ z) * dt)
    return torch.exp(torch.stack(w_inc).sum())

8) Probe‑count controller via jackknife
- Split m probes, compute leave‑one‑out estimates, estimate probe‑induced variance; increase m until relative SE below threshold.

Sketch:

def adapt_probes(estimates_per_probe, rel_tol=0.1, m_max=64):
    m = estimates_per_probe.shape[0]
    mean = estimates_per_probe.mean(0)
    diffs = estimates_per_probe - mean
    se = diffs.pow(2).mean(0).sqrt() / m**0.5
    if (se / (mean.abs() + 1e-8)).max() > rel_tol and m < m_max:
        return int(min(m_max, max(m+4, m*3//2)))
    return m

9) Metrics, logging, diagnostics
- Track: accuracy vs references, variance and CVR for each coefficient; runtime breakdown (JVPs, GP/RPC, critic train, SDE sim); RPC residual vs nodes; active rank spectra; per‑level MLMC variances; probe counts; CV gaps; sparsity PR.
- Use bootstrap 95% CIs over seeds; for sparsity screening, BH‑corrected p‑values with Hutchinson null.

Experiment 1: Synthetic linear/nonlinear SDEs with ground‑truth or high‑precision references
Objective and hypotheses
- Validate correctness and variance gains of coefficient targeting, JVP‑only bracket probing, diffusion‑subspace restriction, probe alignment, and coefficient‑specific critics.
- H1: K=2/3 coefficients reconstruct E[g(X_T)] with O(T^{K+1}) residual and near‑zero bias as Δt→0.
- H2: Runtime scales with effective rank r and probes m≈O(log r), not O(d^2).
- H3: Critics reduce variance ≥10× on nontrivial coefficients; RPC cuts node count ≥3× at fixed tolerance.

Models and observables
- Case A (commuting diffusions, sparsity check):
  • d ∈ {256, 2000, 10000}, r=8. σ_j(x) = e_j f_j(x_j) with smooth bounded f_j; b(x)=−Λx + c, Λ diagonal.
  • Observable g(x)=x^T Q x with sparse banded Q.
  • Many brackets vanish: [σ_i, σ_j]=0 for i≠j, [σ_i, [σ_j, σ_k]]=0.
- Case B (non‑commuting, low‑rank linear, analytic reference for small d):
  • d ∈ {16, 64}, r ∈ {2, 4}. Stratonovich linear fields b(x)=A x, σ_k(x)=B_k x.
  • Observable g(x)=x^T Q x. Analytic E[xx^T] via Lyapunov ODE; small‑T bracket coefficients via commutators [B_i, B_j], [A, B_i].
- Case C (nonlinear, high‑d scaling):
  • d ∈ {512, 2000}, r ∈ {4, 8}. b(x)=−Λx + η tanh(Cx), σ(x)=U diag(s(x)), s_i(x)=sqrt(a_i + b_i x_i^2), U orthonormal d×r.
  • Observables: g2(x)=x^T Q x; g3(x)=∑ w_i x_i^3 (exercise K=3).

Ground truth/reference
- Case A: Zero coefficients identified analytically; nonzeros via high‑precision MC (N≈1e7, Δt=2^−12) or MLMC reference.
- Case B: Exact E[g(X_T)] via matrix ODE; for small T, compute order‑2 bracket coefficients analytically and compare term‑wise.
- Case C: High‑precision MLMC (L=6, tuned N_l) for E[g(X_T)] to validate reconstructed expectations.

Compared methods
- HiLo‑STeP++ full: JVP DLP + diffusion subspace + Oja alignment + Hutch++ + multi‑task RPC + coefficient‑specific critics + time randomization + MLMC coupling.
- Ablations: remove one component at a time (no RPC; no critics; no subspace restriction; no alignment; Hutchinson only; no MLMC/time‑rand; single‑task critics; no bracket‑sparsity screening; naive Gaussian probes).
- Dense‑Jac baseline (d≤64): explicitly form Jacobians/Hessians for brackets and coefficients.

Key procedures and settings
- Bracket‑sparsity screening (Case A): For each candidate node (t, x) from occupancy prior, run 4–8 Hutchinson probes on each basis element; compute two‑sided t‑test of mean contraction vs 0; BH FDR 5%; prune failing brackets before full evaluation.
- Diffusion subspace: randomized range finder with q=16 probes, one power iteration; retain 99% energy. Record r_hat and spectrum.
- Probes: start m=8; adapt by jackknife to reach relative SE < 10% or m_max=64.
- RPC: τ=1e−3 (Case A), 3e−3 (Case C); candidates per time grid: 1024; select ≤256 nodes. Use multi‑task GP with ICM.
- Critics: trunk width 128, 2–3 layers; head width 32; Adam 1e−3; Stein warm‑start 250 steps; then 1–3k detach‑style steps; Neumann depth 2; diffusion‑subspace preconditioning.
- MLMC: base step Δt0=T/4, L≤5; time randomization on; allocate via pilot.

Evaluation metrics and analysis
- Accuracy: absolute/relative error of each coefficient and reconstructed E[g(X_T)] using K=2/3; log–log slope vs T in {0.05, 0.1, 0.2}; expect slope K+1 within ±0.2.
- Variance: per‑coefficient sample variance; CVR = Var(no‑CV)/Var(with‑CV); node counts vs τ; probes vs alignment on/off.
- Efficiency: wall‑time to ε‑MSE; scaling vs d and r; number of JVPs; memory; precision/recall of sparsity detection (Case A) with FDR control.

Illustrative driver skeleton (exp1_synthetic.py outline)

# 1) Define vector fields for Case A/B/C (omitted for brevity)
# 2) Build SDE class and reference solvers
# 3) Prepare RPC prior moments m(t), P(t) via linearization or cubature
# 4) Run bracket‑sparsity screening on candidate nodes
# 5) Estimate subspace; compute coefficients with Hutch++ and alignment
# 6) Train critics online; apply CV; run MLMC with time randomization
# 7) Aggregate metrics; plot slopes; save logs


Experiment 2: High‑dimensional Greeks under local‑vol with low‑rank diffusion
Objective and hypotheses
- Show computational/statistical gains for parameter and initial‑value sensitivities up to d=2000 with r≪d.
- H1: Lower MSE/time than pathwise and LR estimators; scaling with r not d.
- H2: Critics and multi‑task RPC reduce variance/compute ≥5× with MLMC/time‑rand preserving bias.

Model and observables
- Assets S_t ∈ R^d with Itô dynamics: dS_t = diag(S_t)[(μ − 0.5 v(S_t; θ))dt + L(S_t; θ) dW_t], L(S; θ)=U diag(ℓ(S; θ)), U d×r orthonormal.
- ℓ_i(S; θ)=σ0_i (1 + α_i tanh(β_i (log S_i − m_i))). v = diag(LL^T).
- Payoff: basket call g(S_T)=max((1/d)∑ w_i S_i − K, 0). Smooth with ε for pathwise differentiation; report Richardson‑extrapolated ε→0.
- Greeks: Delta along a given direction u; Vega wrt θ=(α, β); optionally rho (μ sensitivity).

HiLo‑STeP++ setup for Greeks
- Observable‑aware mapping: choose Taylor coefficients impacting ∂_x E[g] and ∂_θ E[g]; compute C_k and dC_k/dθ via shared JVP infrastructure.
- Implementation: Use the same path samples and probe seeds to co‑estimate C_k and their θ‑derivatives to reduce variance of the Greek estimator sums.
- RPC: prior moments via lognormal approximation: m(t)=E[S_t], P(t) from assumed‑density evolution under linearized local vol; whiten (t, S) for kernel; multi‑task over Greek‑specific coefficients.
- Critics: shared trunk with per‑Greek heads; diffusion subspace preconditioning; Stein warm‑start 500 steps; online 2k steps.
- MLMC/time randomization: L≤5; adaptive allocation; coupled Brownian and probe seeds across levels.
- Drift tilting (optional): small tilt toward large |h_c| regions identified by proxy; monitor ESS via weight variance; disable if ESS falls below threshold.

Baselines
- Pathwise with payoff smoothing; LR (score‑function) estimator; generator‑gradient baseline (if available) with dense flows for small d; finite differences for θ in small d.

Hyperparameters
- T=0.5; K=2 (with K=1 as ablation); Δt0=T/4; ε_smooth=1e−3 with extrapolation.
- RPC τ=2e−3; max nodes 128; m_init=8, Oja alignment on.
- Critics trunk 256×3; heads 64; Adam with cosine LR 1e−3→1e−4.

Metrics and success criteria
- MSE vs time for each Greek and method; scaling vs d ∈ {256, 1000, 2000} and r ∈ {3, 5}.
- CVR per Greek; node/probe counts; stability across seeds.
- Success: at d=1000, r=5, ≥5× lower MSE/time than best baseline; clear advantage of K=2 over K=1.

Sketch: computing Greek via coefficient reconstruction

# Suppose we have estimates {C_k} and derivatives {dCk_dtheta}
# Greek ≈ sum_{k=0}^K dCk_dtheta

def greek_from_coeffs(C_list, dC_dtheta_list):
    return sum(dC_dtheta_list)

# Shared sampling/seeds to reduce variance
# dCk_dtheta computed by differentiating JVP‑based bracket functionals wrt θ using PyTorch autograd


Experiment 3: Robustness under non‑smooth drifts with mollification and Stein de‑biasing
Objective and hypotheses
- Validate stability under piecewise‑smooth drifts (e.g., ReLU nets) where classical estimators degrade.
- H1: Light Gaussian mollification + Stein de‑biasing controls bias without exploding variance; critics further reduce variance.

Model and observables
- d ∈ {128, 512}, r=5. Drift b(x)=W2 ReLU(W1 x) − λ x with fixed random W1, W2; σ(x)=U diag(√(a + b ⊙ x^2)), Stratonovich forms used for brackets.
- Observables: g1(x)=x^T Q x; g2(x)=tanh(v^T x); g3(x)=max(u^T x, 0) with ε‑smoothing for reporting.

Methods compared
- HiLo‑STeP++ w/ (a) no mollification; (b) Gaussian mollification x→x+ζ, ζ~N(0, s^2 I) in drift evaluations only; (c) (b) + Stein de‑biasing using Gaussian prior score under N(m(t), P(t)).
- Baselines: Pathwise and LR where applicable; generator‑gradient baseline if available.

Protocol and settings
- s ∈ {0, 0.01, 0.02, 0.05}. For each s: estimate coefficients (K=2) and reconstruct E[g(X_T)] with fixed compute budget; report bias/variance/MSE.
- Stein de‑biasing: score(x; t) = P(t)^{-1}(x − m(t)); add first‑order correction Δ ≈ E[∇·(score c) + score·∇c] evaluated under prior at RPC nodes; approximate with MC over prior samples.
- Critics: Stein warm‑start emphasized to avoid 2nd‑order derivatives.
- RPC: τ=3e−3; m_init=10; Δt0=T/8; T=0.3.

Metrics and success criteria
- MSE vs s; bias vs s; variance vs s; runtime overhead.
- Success: with s≈0.02 and Stein de‑biasing, MSE within 1.2× of smooth‑drift baseline; no‑mollification shows ≥10× variance or instability; CVR≥5× with critics.

Sketch: mollification and Stein correction

# Mollified drift evaluation

def mollified_b(b_raw, x, s):
    if s == 0.0: return b_raw(x)
    eps = torch.randn_like(x) * s
    return b_raw(x + eps)

# Stein correction under Gaussian prior N(m, P)

def stein_correction(c_val, grad_c, x, m, P_inv):
    # c_val: N×1, grad_c: N×d, x: N×d
    score = (x - m) @ P_inv.T  # N×d
    # divergence term of score * c ≈ trace(P_inv) * c when prior is Gaussian with fixed P
    div_term = torch.trace(P_inv) * c_val  # scalar times N×1
    inner = (score * grad_c).sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True)
    return (div_term + inner).mean()

Reliability enhancements and controls (applies to all experiments)
- Sanity checks on JVP engine: compare [V, W] via JVP vs finite‑difference Jacobian‑vector products in d=4 toy problems; assert relative error < 1e−3.
- Stratonovich converter validation: for simple σ(x)=const and linear σ, verify against closed forms.
- Probe alignment A/B tests: run with/without Oja alignment at fixed m; check variance reduction of Hutch estimates.
- MLMC consistency: verify MLMC telescoping sum convergence and level variance decay V_l ~ O(Δt_l^β); report estimated β and compare to theory.
- RPC residual calibration: cross‑validate GP posterior variance as a proxy for interpolation error using holdout nodes; stop when CV error < τ.
- Sparsity screening FDR control: BH at 5% across tested brackets and nodes; report PR curves.
- Confidence intervals: bootstrap across independent RNG seeds for runtime and variance metrics; report 95% CIs.
- Memory and numerics: gradient checkpointing for critics; float32 default with selective float64 for small d references; enable torch.set_float32_matmul_precision('high') when stable.

Ablation toggles and configurations
- Flags: --no-rpc, --no-critics, --no-subspace, --no-align, --no-mlmc, --no-time-rand, --single-task-critics, --no-sparsity-screen.
- Levels: K in {1, 2, 3}; Δt control; probe counts; RPC τ; critic architectures.

Data management and outputs
- Save per‑run JSON/CSV: seeds, d, r, K, τ, m_init, r_hat spectra, nodes selected, per‑level MLMC stats, coefficients and SEs, critics loss curves, CVR, runtime breakdown.
- Plots: error vs T slopes (log–log); MSE/time vs d and r; CVR bars; RPC node counts vs τ; probe counts vs alignment; sparsity PR (Case A); Greeks MSE/time comparisons; MSE vs s (Experiment 3).

Deliverables
- Scripts: exp1_synthetic.py, exp2_greeks.py, exp3_nonsmooth.py.
- Configs: YAML/JSON for d, r, T, K, τ, m_init, MLMC levels, critic sizes.
- Reproducible results with seeds; instructions for pip installs: torchsde, gpytorch, botorch.

Success criteria summary
- Experiment 1: order slopes ≈ K+1; ≥5× runtime improvement vs dense‑Jac at d=64; CVR≥10× on ≥50% nonzero coefficients; ≥95% correct pruning with <1% false positives.
- Experiment 2: at d=1000, r=5, ≥5× lower MSE/time than best baseline; improved stability and lower gradient variance; K=2 outperforms K=1.
- Experiment 3: with s≈0.02 + Stein correction, MSE within 1.2× smooth baseline; no‑mollification unstable; critics yield CVR≥5×.

Notes on practicality
- If torchsde is unavailable, provide a fallback EM/Heun solver wrapper using torchdiffeq‑style steps; however, the default setup will use torchsde to avoid custom solvers.
- BoTorch fantasy models accelerate RPC greedy selection without refitting from scratch.
- Krylov caching: cache probe‑aligned subspaces and JVP results keyed by nearby nodes (t, x) buckets to amortize costs across MLMC levels.

Example end‑to‑end pseudocode snippet (tying major components)

# Pseudocode for a single coefficient estimation loop (shared for Experiments 1–3)
for level in range(L+1):
    dt = T / (2**level)
    # 1) Draw batch of paths with shared seeds; sample τ ~ Unif(0, T)
    tau = torch.rand(N_level, 1, device=device) * T
    x_tau = simulate_path_to_tau(sde, x0_batch, tau, dt, shared_brownian=seed_base+level)
    # 2) RPC candidate nodes: build (t, x) pool using prior N(m(t), P(t)); select nodes greedily with GP
    Xcand = make_candidates_from_prior(m_t_fn, P_t_fn, times_grid, M=2048)
    new_nodes = select_rpc_nodes(gp_model, Xcand, K_add=16, tasks=coeff_tasks, tau=rpc_tau, current_nodes=current_nodes)
    current_nodes = torch.cat([current_nodes, new_nodes], dim=0)
    # 3) At selected nodes, estimate diffusion subspace Ur and aligned probes
    for node in current_nodes:
        Ur = estimate_diffusion_subspace(sigmas, node_x, q=16)
        probes = torch.randn(d, m_init, device=device)
        for _ in range(3):
            probes = oja_update(probes, lambda Z: bracket_op_apply(Z, node), lr=0.1)
        # Hutch++ contractions restricted to Ur
        trace_est = hutchpp_trace(lambda Z: restricted_op_apply(Z, Ur), d=Ur.shape[0], m=m_init, Q_init=probes)
        # Jackknife adapt m if needed
    # 4) Critics: warm‑start with Stein; then minimize Poisson residual (detach‑style)
    train_critics_on_nodes(critic, current_nodes, h_targets, b, sigmas, Ur_list)
    # 5) Coefficient estimates with control variates and MLMC telescoping
    coeff_estimates[level] = estimate_coefficients_with_cv(paths_level, critics, probes, nodes)
# Combine levels; compute dθ estimates if required; aggregate metrics

This experimental plan operationalizes all items in the Experiment Strategy with detailed procedures, code sketches, hyperparameters, evaluation metrics, and reliability controls, using PyTorch and established libraries to avoid from‑scratch implementations while fully exercising the HiLo‑STeP++ methodology.
experimental_design.experiment_code:
# HiLo-STeP++: Experimental code (PyTorch-only core) for 3 experiments
# - Implements JVP-only Lie-bracket probing, diffusion-subspace restriction,
#   randomized contractions (Hutchinson/Hutch++-style), simple RPC-like node selection,
#   multi-head critics (Poisson-style control variates), time randomization, and a minimal MLMC coupling.
# - Includes three experiments:
#   1) Synthetic SDEs (commuting vs non-commuting vs nonlinear) with bracket-sparsity screening and CV variance reduction.
#   2) High-dimensional Greeks (Delta) under low-rank local volatility with control variates.
#   3) Robustness under non-smooth drifts with Gaussian mollification and Stein de-biasing.
# - Produces .pdf plots suitable for inclusion in academic papers.
# - Adds a quick test() that runs tiny versions for functionality.
#
# Notes:
# - For simplicity and portability, this script uses a custom Euler–Maruyama simulator and avoids external SDE libraries.
# - RPC is approximated with an occupancy-aware, proxy-variance-based greedy node selector.
# - Critics use a detach-style Poisson residual loss that avoids Hessians.
# - Hutch++ and Oja alignment are demonstrated in simplified forms tailored to vector-field brackets.
#
# Required Python libraries (install if missing):
# - torch (PyTorch 2.x)
# - numpy
# - matplotlib
# - seaborn
# - (standard) time, math, json, random

import math
import time
import json
import random
from typing import Callable, List, Tuple, Dict

import numpy as np
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch.autograd.functional import jvp
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use("Agg")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns


# -----------------------------
# Utilities and global settings
# -----------------------------

def set_seed(seed: int = 42):
    random.seed(seed)
    np.random.seed(seed)
    torch.manual_seed(seed)

def get_device():
    return torch.device("cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")


torch.set_float32_matmul_precision("high")


# ----------------------------------------------------
# JVP-only Stratonovich correction and Lie brackets
# ----------------------------------------------------
# Vector fields V, W: Callable[x: Tensor(d,)] -> Tensor(d,)

def stratonovich_drift(b: Callable, sigmas: List[Callable], x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    # b^∘(x) = b(x) - 0.5 sum_j J(sigma_j)(x) sigma_j(x)
    corr = torch.zeros_like(x)
    for sj in sigmas:
        v = sj(x)
        _, jvp_val = jvp(sj, (x,), (v,), create_graph=False, strict=True)
        corr = corr + 0.5 * jvp_val
    return b(x) - corr


def lie_bracket(V: Callable, W: Callable, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    # [V, W](x) = J_W(x) V(x) - J_V(x) W(x)
    Vx = V(x)
    Wx = W(x)
    _, JW_Vx = jvp(W, (x,), (Vx,), create_graph=False, strict=True)
    _, JV_Wx = jvp(V, (x,), (Wx,), create_graph=False, strict=True)
    return JW_Vx - JV_Wx


def nested_bracket(V: Callable, W: Callable, U: Callable, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    def inner(z):
        return lie_bracket(W, U, z)
    return lie_bracket(V, inner, x)


# ----------------------------------------------------
# Diffusion-subspace restriction and probe alignment
# ----------------------------------------------------

def estimate_diffusion_subspace(sigmas: List[Callable], x: torch.Tensor, q: int = 16, power_iter: int = 1,
                                energy_thresh: float = 0.99) -> torch.Tensor:
    # Build S = [sigma_1(x) ... sigma_r(x)] in R^{d×r}
    S = torch.stack([sj(x) for sj in sigmas], dim=1)  # d × r
    d, r_full = S.shape
    if r_full == 0:
        return torch.eye(d, device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
    # Randomized range finder
    q_eff = min(q, r_full)
    Omega = torch.randn(r_full, q_eff, device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
    Y = S @ Omega
    for _ in range(power_iter):
        Y = S @ (S.T @ Y)
    Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(Y, mode='reduced')  # d × q_eff
    B = Q.T @ S
    # SVD on small matrix
    U_s, Sv, _ = torch.linalg.svd(B, full_matrices=False)
    if torch.sum(Sv**2) <= 1e-12:
        return Q @ U_s  # arbitrary
    energy = (Sv**2).cumsum(0) / (Sv**2).sum()
    r_hat = int(torch.searchsorted(energy, torch.tensor(energy_thresh, device=energy.device)).item() + 1)
    Ur = (Q @ U_s[:, :r_hat])  # d × r_hat
    return Ur


@torch.no_grad()
def oja_update(probes: torch.Tensor, op_apply: Callable[[torch.Tensor], torch.Tensor], lr: float = 0.1, iters: int = 2) -> torch.Tensor:
    # probes: d × m
    Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(probes, mode='reduced')
    P = Q.clone()
    for _ in range(iters):
        Y = op_apply(P)
        P = P + lr * Y
        P, _ = torch.linalg.qr(P, mode='reduced')
    return P


# ----------------------------------------------------
# Randomized contractions (Hutchinson/Hutch++)
# ----------------------------------------------------

def hutchinson_norm_sq(vec: torch.Tensor, m: int = 8) -> torch.Tensor:
    # Estimates ||vec||^2 via E[(z·vec)^2], z~N(0,I): unbiased for Euclidean norm
    d = vec.numel()
    Z = torch.randn(d, m, device=vec.device, dtype=vec.dtype)
    proj = (Z * vec[:, None]).sum(dim=0)
    return (proj.pow(2).mean())


def hutchpp_trace_symmetric(op_mul: Callable[[torch.Tensor], torch.Tensor], d: int, m: int = 8, Q_init: torch.Tensor = None) -> torch.Tensor:
    # op_mul acts on d×k -> d×k, symmetric operator
    G = torch.randn(d, m, device=Q_init.device if Q_init is not None else None)
    Y = op_mul(G)
    Q, _ = torch.linalg.qr(Y, mode='reduced')
    if Q_init is not None:
        Q = torch.linalg.qr(torch.cat([Q, Q_init], dim=1), mode='reduced')[0]
    B = Q.T @ op_mul(Q)
    Z = torch.randn(d, m, device=Q.device, dtype=Q.dtype)
    PZ = Z - Q @ (Q.T @ Z)
    R = op_mul(PZ)
    trace_resid = torch.sum(PZ * R) / m
    return torch.trace(B) + trace_resid


# ----------------------------------------------------
# Simple Euler–Maruyama SDE simulator (Itô)
# ----------------------------------------------------

class ItoSDE:
    def __init__(self, b: Callable[[torch.Tensor], torch.Tensor], sigmas: List[Callable[[torch.Tensor], torch.Tensor]], r: int):
        self.b = b
        self.sigmas = sigmas
        self.r = r

    def sigma_matrix(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        if self.r == 0:
            return torch.zeros(x.numel(), 0, device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
        return torch.stack([sj(x) for sj in self.sigmas], dim=1)  # d×r

    def simulate_to_time(self, x0: torch.Tensor, tau: float, dt: float, seed: int = 0) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        # Returns x_tau, last_dW_sum (r,), total_steps
        torch.manual_seed(seed)
        x = x0.clone()
        steps = max(1, int(math.ceil(tau / dt)))
        d = x0.numel()
        last_dW_sum = torch.zeros(self.r, device=x0.device, dtype=x0.dtype)
        for k in range(steps):
            t_cur = min((k + 1) * dt, tau)
            h = dt if k < steps - 1 else (tau - k * dt)
            drift = self.b(x)
            if self.r > 0:
                S = self.sigma_matrix(x)
                dW = torch.randn(self.r, device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype) * math.sqrt(h)
                x = x + drift * h + S @ dW
                last_dW_sum = last_dW_sum + dW
            else:
                x = x + drift * h
        return x, last_dW_sum, torch.tensor(steps)

    def simulate_final(self, x0: torch.Tensor, T: float, dt: float, seed: int = 0) -> torch.Tensor:
        xT, _, _ = self.simulate_to_time(x0, T, dt, seed)
        return xT


# ----------------------------------------------------
# Observables and gradients
# ----------------------------------------------------

def make_quadratic_Q(d: int, bandwidth: int = 3, scale: float = 0.5, seed: int = 0) -> torch.Tensor:
    torch.manual_seed(seed)
    Q = torch.zeros(d, d)
    for i in range(d):
        for j in range(max(0, i - bandwidth), min(d, i + bandwidth + 1)):
            Q[i, j] = torch.randn(()) * scale / (1 + abs(i - j))
    Q = (Q + Q.T) / 2.0
    return Q


def g_quadratic(x: torch.Tensor, Q: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    return (x * (Q @ x)).sum()


def grad_g_quadratic(x: torch.Tensor, Q: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    return (Q + Q.T) @ x


def g_cubic(x: torch.Tensor, w: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    # sum w_i x_i^3
    return (w * x.pow(3)).sum()


def grad_g_cubic(x: torch.Tensor, w: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
    return 3.0 * w * x.pow(2)


# ----------------------------------------------------
# Integrands based on Lie brackets and observables
# ----------------------------------------------------

def integrand_h_total(x: torch.Tensor, b: Callable, sigmas: List[Callable], grad_g: Callable[[torch.Tensor], torch.Tensor],
                      pair_probes: int = 4) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, Dict[str, torch.Tensor]]:
    # h_total = sum_i grad_g(x)·[b, sigma_i](x)  + randomized estimate of sum_{i<j} grad_g(x)·[sigma_i, sigma_j](x)
    ggrad = grad_g(x)
    r = len(sigmas)
    # sum over i: [b, sigma_i]
    h1 = torch.zeros((), device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
    for i in range(r):
        br = lie_bracket(b, sigmas[i], x)
        h1 = h1 + (ggrad * br).sum()
    # randomized contractions for sigma-sigma brackets
    h2 = torch.zeros((), device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
    if r >= 2 and pair_probes > 0:
        for _ in range(pair_probes):
            alpha = torch.randn(r, device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
            beta = torch.randn(r, device=x.device, dtype=x.dtype)
            def V(z):
                return sum(alpha[j] * sigmas[j](z) for j in range(r))
            def W(z):
                return sum(beta[j] * sigmas[j](z) for j in range(r))
            br_vw = lie_bracket(V, W, x)
            h2 = h2 + (ggrad * br_vw).sum()
        h2 = h2 / pair_probes
    h_total = h1 + h2
    parts = {"h_bsigma": h1.detach(), "h_sigmasigma": h2.detach()}
    return h_total, parts


# ----------------------------------------------------
# Critics (multi-head shared trunk) and Poisson residual loss (detach-style)
# ----------------------------------------------------

class CriticNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d: int, C: int, hidden: int = 128, layers: int = 2):
        super().__init__()
        mods = [nn.Linear(d + 1, hidden), nn.SiLU()]
        for _ in range(layers - 1):
            mods += [nn.Linear(hidden, hidden), nn.SiLU()]
        self.trunk = nn.Sequential(*mods)
        self.val_heads = nn.ModuleList([nn.Linear(hidden, 1) for _ in range(C)])
        self.grad_heads = nn.ModuleList([nn.Linear(hidden, d) for _ in range(C)])

    def forward(self, t: torch.Tensor, x: torch.Tensor) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, List[torch.Tensor]]:
        h = self.trunk(torch.cat([t, x], dim=-1))
        vals = torch.cat([hd(h) for hd in self.val_heads], dim=-1)  # N×C
        grads = [gh(h) for gh in self.grad_heads]                   # list of N×d
        return vals, grads


def poisson_residual_loss(critic: CriticNet, batch_t: torch.Tensor, batch_x: torch.Tensor,
                          h_targets: torch.Tensor, b: Callable, Ur: torch.Tensor = None,
                          head_indices: List[int] = None) -> torch.Tensor:
    # L c ≈ b·∇c (diffusion term omitted) and match to h
    vals, grads_list = critic(batch_t, batch_x)
    C = h_targets.shape[1]
    loss = torch.zeros((), device=batch_x.device)
    for ci in range(C):
        if head_indices is not None:
            hi = head_indices[ci]
        else:
            hi = ci
        grads = grads_list[hi]
        if Ur is not None:
            grads = grads @ (Ur @ Ur.T)
        drift_term = (b(batch_x.T).T if batch_x.ndim == 2 else b(batch_x))  # tolerate shapes
        if batch_x.ndim == 2:
            drift_term = torch.stack([b(batch_x[i]) for i in range(batch_x.shape[0])], dim=0)
        residual = (drift_term * grads).sum(dim=-1, keepdim=True) - h_targets[:, ci:ci+1]
        loss = loss + (residual.pow(2).mean())
    return loss / C


# ----------------------------------------------------
# RPC-like occupancy-aware node selection
# ----------------------------------------------------

def sample_prior_states(d: int, N: int, mean: torch.Tensor, cov_diag: torch.Tensor, device) -> torch.Tensor:
    z = torch.randn(N, d, device=device) * torch.sqrt(cov_diag)[None, :]
    return mean[None, :] + z


def rpc_select_nodes(times: torch.Tensor, d: int, num_candidates_per_t: int, num_select: int,
                     proxy_fn: Callable[[torch.Tensor, float], torch.Tensor],
                     mean_fn: Callable[[float], torch.Tensor],
                     cov_diag_fn: Callable[[float], torch.Tensor], device) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    # Greedy selection by proxy variance score
    cand_nodes_t = []
    cand_nodes_x = []
    scores = []
    for t in times:
        m = mean_fn(float(t.item()))
        Pdiag = cov_diag_fn(float(t.item()))
        Xcand = sample_prior_states(d, num_candidates_per_t, m, Pdiag, device)
        sc = proxy_fn(Xcand, float(t.item()))  # N
        cand_nodes_t.append(torch.full((num_candidates_per_t, 1), float(t.item()), device=device))
        cand_nodes_x.append(Xcand)
        scores.append(sc)
    Tnodes = torch.cat(cand_nodes_t, dim=0)
    Xnodes = torch.cat(cand_nodes_x, dim=0)
    Scores = torch.cat(scores, dim=0)
    # Select top-scoring nodes
    idx = torch.topk(Scores.flatten(), k=min(num_select, Scores.numel())).indices
    return Tnodes[idx], Xnodes[idx]


# ----------------------------------------------------
# Bracket-sparsity screening
# ----------------------------------------------------

def bracket_sparsity_screen(sigmas: List[Callable], x: torch.Tensor, thresh: float = 1e-6, mprobes: int = 8) -> Dict[str, float]:
    r = len(sigmas)
    zero_pairs = 0
    total_pairs = max(0, r * (r - 1) // 2)
    magnitudes = []
    if total_pairs == 0:
        return {"precision": 1.0, "recall": 1.0, "avg_magnitude": 0.0}
    for i in range(r):
        for j in range(i + 1, r):
            br = lie_bracket(sigmas[i], sigmas[j], x)
            mag = hutchinson_norm_sq(br, m=mprobes).item()
            magnitudes.append(mag)
            if mag < thresh:
                zero_pairs += 1
    precision = zero_pairs / total_pairs
    recall = precision  # here, ground truth for Case A is all zero; for general, treat equally
    avg_mag = float(np.mean(magnitudes)) if len(magnitudes) > 0 else 0.0
    return {"precision": precision, "recall": recall, "avg_magnitude": avg_mag}


# ----------------------------------------------------
# Simple MLMC with two levels (coarse/fine) and time randomization
# ----------------------------------------------------

def mlmc_two_level_estimator(sde: ItoSDE, x0: torch.Tensor, T: float, dt_coarse: float, dt_fine: float,
                             N_coarse: int, N_fine: int,
                             integrand: Callable[[torch.Tensor], torch.Tensor],
                             seed_base: int = 0) -> Tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
    device = x0.device
    # Fine level
    vals_fine = []
    for n in range(N_fine):
        tau = torch.rand(1, device=device).item() * T
        xf, _, _ = sde.simulate_to_time(x0, tau, dt_fine, seed=seed_base + n)
        vals_fine.append(integrand(xf).detach())
    fine_mean = torch.stack(vals_fine).mean()
    # Coarse level (control variate via coupled seeds)
    deltas = []
    for n in range(N_coarse):
        tau = torch.rand(1, device=device).item() * T
        xf, _, _ = sde.simulate_to_time(x0, tau, dt_fine, seed=seed_base + n)   # fine
        xc, _, _ = sde.simulate_to_time(x0, tau, dt_coarse, seed=seed_base + n)  # coarse coupled
        deltas.append((integrand(xf) - integrand(xc)).detach())
    delta_mean = torch.stack(deltas).mean()
    estimate = T * (fine_mean + delta_mean)
    # crude variance estimates
    fine_var = torch.stack(vals_fine).var(unbiased=True) if len(vals_fine) > 1 else torch.tensor(0.0, device=device)
    delta_var = torch.stack(deltas).var(unbiased=True) if len(deltas) > 1 else torch.tensor(0.0, device=device)
    return estimate, fine_var, delta_var


# ----------------------------------------------------
# Experiment 1: Synthetic SDEs
# ----------------------------------------------------

def experiment1_synthetic(device=None, fast: bool = False):
    print("\n=== Experiment 1: Synthetic SDEs (brackets, CV variance reduction, sparsity) ===")
    device = get_device() if device is None else device
    torch.set_default_dtype(torch.float32)

    # Settings (fast mode trims sizes)
    # Case A (commuting), Case B (non-commuting linear), Case C (nonlinear low-rank)
    configs = [
        {"name": "CaseA_commuting", "d": 64 if not fast else 16, "r": 6, "T": 0.2, "dt_fine": 0.005, "dt_coarse": 0.01},
        {"name": "CaseB_linear",    "d": 32 if not fast else 12, "r": 3, "T": 0.2, "dt_fine": 0.005, "dt_coarse": 0.01},
        {"name": "CaseC_nonlinear", "d": 128 if not fast else 24, "r": 4, "T": 0.2, "dt_fine": 0.005, "dt_coarse": 0.01},
    ]

    # Critic training config
    critic_steps = 800 if not fast else 200
    critic_hidden = 128
    batch_nodes = 64 if not fast else 32
    rpc_select = 96 if not fast else 32
    pair_probes = 6 if not fast else 3

    # Storage for plotting
    losses_all = {}
    cvr_all = {}
    bracket_stats = {}

    for cfg in configs:
        name = cfg["name"]
        d = cfg["d"]; r = cfg["r"]; T = cfg["T"]
        dt_fine = cfg["dt_fine"]; dt_coarse = cfg["dt_coarse"]
        print(f"\n-- {name}: d={d}, r={r}, T={T}")
        set_seed(123)

        # Build vector fields
        if name.startswith("CaseA"):  # commuting
            Lambda = torch.linspace(0.1, 0.3, d, device=device)
            cvec = torch.linspace(-0.5, 0.5, d, device=device) * 0.1
            b = lambda x: -Lambda * x + cvec
            # choose distinct coords for r columns
            idxs = torch.randperm(d)[:r].tolist()
            funcs = []
            for j, k in enumerate(idxs):
                # sigma_j only acts on coord k via smooth fn f_j
                def make_sigma(kidx, amp):
                    def sj(z):
                        v = torch.zeros_like(z)
                        v[kidx] = amp * torch.tanh(z[kidx])
                        return v
                    return sj
                funcs.append(make_sigma(k, 0.4 + 0.15 * torch.randn(()).abs().item()))
            sigmas = funcs
            # observable g: quadratic with banded Q
            Q = make_quadratic_Q(d, bandwidth=3, scale=0.3, seed=7).to(device)
            grad_g = lambda x: grad_g_quadratic(x, Q)
        elif name.startswith("CaseB"):  # linear non-commuting
            A = torch.randn(d, d, device=device) * 0.1
            def b(x, A=A):
                return A @ x
            Bmats = [torch.randn(d, d, device=device) * 0.2 for _ in range(r)]
            sigmas = [lambda x, B=Bm: B @ x for Bm in Bmats]
            Q = make_quadratic_Q(d, bandwidth=2, scale=0.5, seed=11).to(device)
            grad_g = lambda x: grad_g_quadratic(x, Q)
        else:  # Case C nonlinear low-rank
            Lambda = torch.linspace(0.05, 0.2, d, device=device)
            Cmat = torch.randn(d, d, device=device) / math.sqrt(d)
            eta = 0.6
            b = lambda x: -Lambda * x + eta * torch.tanh(Cmat @ x)
            # low-rank diffusion sigma_j(x) = U[:,j] * s_j(u_j^T x)
            U = torch.linalg.qr(torch.randn(d, r, device=device), mode='reduced')[0]
            a = 0.2 + 0.05 * torch.rand(r, device=device)
            bb = 0.1 + 0.05 * torch.rand(r, device=device)
            ucols = [U[:, j] for j in range(r)]
            def make_sigma(uvec, ai, bi):
                def sj(z):
                    proj = (uvec * z).sum()
                    scale = torch.sqrt(ai + bi * proj.pow(2))
                    return uvec * scale
                return sj
            sigmas = [make_sigma(ucols[j], a[j], bb[j]) for j in range(r)]
            w = torch.randn(d, device=device) * 0.2
            grad_g = lambda x: grad_g_cubic(x, w)

        # SDE object
